title: Senators John Fetterman and Dave McCormick: Bipartisanship, Money in DC, Datacenters, Graham Platner

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Now a look at the state of Pennsylvania. It's one of the most important states in this presidential race.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Both of Pennsylvania's senators, Democrat John Fetterman and Republican Dave McCormick, voted in favor of the bill.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: In this situation, we both agreed that shutting our government down is wrong for our commonwealth and for our nation.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: So we're looking for ways to find common ground. And when we disagree, we disagree, but when we can agree, we agree.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Our votes are about country over party at this point.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Well, we're trying to set a good example because we have lots of disagreements, but we trust each other, we like one another, and we look for ways to work together.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I thought the best way to kick this off was to actually give both of you a few moments to just say what's on your mind. And so I think in the rules of parliamentary decorum, Senator Fetterman as a senior sitting senator from Pennsylvania, maybe you can start.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: I'm proud to call Senator McCormick a good friend. And how things are very more polarized and how I refuse to engage in the kinds of extremism. And we're going to fight together for Pennsylvania and for our nation right now. And we're going to continue to have conversations like this, whether in Pennsylvania or here. So we have to find a better way forward through a lot of the circumstances.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Dave?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think, I mean, one of the benefits of being in a place like Pennsylvania is that the voters expect us really to find ways to work together. And if you put this moment in perspective, I think it's probably the most consequential moment in all of our lifetimes. And in fact, I think it might be the most consequential moment in humankind, which is a big statement. So much change is happening, it's artificial intelligence, of course, but it's really how that's affecting everything: life sciences, defense, energy. It's a moment of enormous change, and that change creates a lot of anxiety that lends itself to polarization and extremes. And so if there was ever a time where it's more important to find ways to work together, it's now. And so we've found ways to work together on everything from energy policy to fentanyl, the fentanyl crisis, to anti-Semitism in Pennsylvania and across the country, to drone technology. And these are issues that are of such consequence that you can't get there by just being with your team. You have to be able to find common ground, and we've really looked for ways to do that.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think he could confirm. Pennsylvania keeps you honest now. And now, I'd like to remind everybody that we are the only two people in our respective cycles that flipped...

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Both parties want that. We want to flip seats. It's like, well, you know, we've done that because I think we've rejected the extreme kinds of views, and now it's better to work together for Pennsylvania or for our country for right now. And AI is really, really important, and that will sometimes put me at odds with the direction that the Democratic Party seems to be moving in that. So now, a moratorium for data centers—for me, that's a China-first kind of policy for that. I'm going to continue to push back that. I'm not going to describe that as a scourge. I'm going to describe that as an incredible new opportunity. And now, you know, my friend, he organized an incredible symposium in Western Pennsylvania that brought AI together and energy, and that's two parts of things Pennsylvania really brings to the table in this very, very important conversation.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: I think that we're at this point now where, is it fair to say as goes Pennsylvania, as goes the rest of the country? In the sense that if you can find common ground in the state on these very thorny issues, maybe the rest of the country can find ground. And the vice versa is also true: if you guys can't find resolution, what are the odds of California and West Virginia making it up, finding resolution? So what is it like, a day in the life in the US Senate, trying to find these compromises? Because from our perspective, outside in, a lot of the time it just seems like nothing is happening. Dave.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think if you step back, I know we look very similar, but we have many similar backgrounds...

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: We do?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: We have...

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: What's the difference?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: We have similar backgrounds in the sense we both grew up in rural Pennsylvania and had similar childhoods. John went into public service; I left, went to West Point, went to the military. But we both came back from those rural backgrounds. But what's interesting in Pennsylvania is nineteen electoral votes, and it's a microcosm of the country. You have these two big urban centers, Philadelphia and Pittsburgh, which are mostly Democrat. You have these rural areas that are mostly red, mostly Republican. And you've got to build a coalition across those to win. So ironically, a lot of the same people that voted for Senator Fetterman voted for me and voted for President Trump. And that's the coalition you have to build. And in particular, it is a working families coalition. So the thing that's ironic is that even though all the national unions would have endorsed Kamala Harris and Bob Casey, my opponent, the rank and file—two-thirds of the rank and file, the electricians, the pipe fitters, the steamfitters—voted for me. Same with him; they voted for him. So we actually, even though we...

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: have a lot of differences in our parties, we have come together around that coalition. And that coalition includes a very significant Latino turnout, a very significant African American turnout, the highest African American turnout in 2024 in the last 30 years. And so, that coalition, I think is up for grabs. Who wins that coalition in Pennsylvania and who wins it in the country, I think will dictate the future to a large degree in 2028 and beyond. And so that's what we're working on together because we're trying to build that coalition to do good things together in Pennsylvania.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The Senate is at an all-time low in terms of approval rating. We have the lowest approval rating of a president in history after Biden, who was extremely low rated. You guys can't control the debt. The American populace believe we'd be in a golden age right now, they're not seeing it. Inflation is soaring. The president seems to be running amok, has no check in the house, and you guys are responsible—

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Tell us, tell us what you really think.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Other than, other than that Mrs. Lincoln.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Well, and 40 trillion dollars in debt and you're spending more than ever. So the American people, I think, are extremely frustrated with both parties. It's great to see you guys come together, but what are you going to do to bring back some balance between the branches of the government? Because this does not seem to be going in the right direction for anybody but the people in this room, which is to say the top 10 percent of our society.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Well, and I mean for me, if you reference inflation is soaring, well, just just recently, that's primarily driven by energy prices right now. Now, why are those energy prices high? Well, because the president decided to hold Iran accountable. And now I'm the only Democrat that actually agrees with that. That was not, that was not a radical idea for a Democrat. Every single person running for president as a Democrat said we must never allow Iran to acquire a nuclear bomb. And now we have the opportunity to hold them accountable. Why? That's why I continue to vote and and again against these war powers act now too. So for inflation, yes, now, absolutely no one thinks a five dollars a gallon is is great, but if you allow Iran to become nuclear, you know, that's going to seem quaint, you know, at five dollars a gallon holding them to. So what's driving this economic conversation overall right now? So for me, I think what's most important now, turn everything as a partisan thing and that's the danger where if we despise Trump more than we are concerned about Iran becoming a nuclear power, that will have profound implications for not just in that region, but all over.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: for and a global stage for right now. That's the difficulty moving forward now. I've said this in the podcast, you know, for All-In. I, you know, who's the leader's party? I'm like that's TDS 100%. You know, our party is defined by the opposition of whatever he comes out for. I've said he Trump comes out for ice cream and lazy Sundays, we're we're going to hate that shit. You know, and we're going to vote it down.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Now, we all have a bandwidth and we all have a platform. You know, I feel like you have to discriminate. I don't care about the ballroom. If you want to build it, sure, great. I was there for the White House correspondents' dinner and I saw how dangerous that was for for our leadership. But I don't care about the reflecting pool. But now we can't stop talking about all this other kind of shit so we can really focus on thing that's more important when now it's become all just distract- distracted by these small small ball games.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Dave, is there a path to resolution? I mean, like, you guys are going to be forced to confront the filibuster, which is this critical bulwark that sort of leaves some sort of attempt at finding common ground out there. But there's a lot of pressure to end it. I think Senator Fetterman, you've said you'd be supportive of ending the filibuster. Is that where we're going to just total tribalism?

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Yeah, let's talk about the filibuster. Let's talk about that.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: The entire Democratic party, including myself, we were so wrong about the filibuster. In my cycle in 2020, every single Democrat identified, we have to we have to eliminate the filibuster. Seems like it makes sense now. And thank God, thank God we had people that stood there, whether it's Senator Manchin or Senator Sinema, you know, history has vindicated their wisdom to do that thing. And now Democrats, we all wanted to get rid of it in '22 and 2025, we fucking love it. And now it forces people to work together with both sides. You know, if you turn the Senate into a smaller version of the House and majority majority rules, now that would have profound profound implications for now. So someone that would be the first person to announce, 'Hey, I was so wrong about that.' Oh, that's one of the kinds of hill I would die on now to defend the filibuster, how important it is to the Senate, but also Senate for the minority rights, whoever that happens to be in in that time. You know, that's what's so critical what the filibuster is to governance here.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Well, I would say, um, first of all, Congress, but lots of institutions right now have very low public approval, very low trust. That's a huge problem. It's across it's across all areas, the church, public officials, business, in many cases. So we've got to rebuild trust in our institutions.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: institutions and one of the ways you do that is through your own personal conduct. So one of the things we can do is try to set the example. Uh, if you look at my voting and his voting, I - I am an unapologetic conservative. I vote as a conservative. At the same time, that doesn't mean you can't work together. So we look for every opportunity for bipartisan legislation. So I've got more bipartisan legislation than anybody. We've collaborated on dozens of things together because you can do both. And the filibuster, I mean, it is frustrating as a business person, I was in business for 25 years, so frustrating the way - the pace of how slowly things move. But I do think it creates the requirement, the impetus, to get things done.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: And I would disagree with you. I think a lot of big things have been done. I think if you look at the energy agenda, we can't win the AI race without energy, and uh, we've made huge progress in energy. I'm hoping we're going to get permitting reform, which would be the biggest economic lever we could possibly get done over the next six months or the next two and a half years. Um, we've closed the border, we've stopped the flow of fentanyl. We had 4,000 people who died in Pennsylvania in the last year of the Biden administration; it's 1,600 this year. It's down 60%. Those same statistics nationally. And then there's huge intractable problems like the debt and the deficit, which are existential, that we're not dealing with. And that's not a product so much of the filibuster or not the filibuster. That's a product of the polarization of our country and both sides taking advantage of anybody willing to - to look at a problem in a - in a hard way.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: But my question was though about the bottom half of the country in the K-shaped economy we're in, and how it's not working, and you guys are phenomenally unpopular with that group of Americans. So is there a way to make this economy...

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: You talk about wealth concentration?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, wealth concentration, the bottom half is not happy with - on both sides.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: No, no. See, it's a huge - no, it's a huge problem. And I - I don't think I listened carefully last night. I mean, I just want to tell you, uh, that is a huge, huge problem that's growing. The median income in Pennsylvania is $52,000 a year. When you guys say AI or AI anxiety, I mean, these - these folks, they've been down this road once before when a bunch of smart, rich people said we got the answer, it's called globalization. And what happened in my hometown is there was a mill of 2,000 people that got gutted. It's down to 100 people. So there's enormous anxiety, but it's because there's no path to getting ahead. And everybody in this room, um, the last 10 years has been the greatest 10 years in the history of humanity for those who have assets.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So do you have a plan?

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well there's - there's gotta be a number of plans.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What is it?

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Because it's not just going to be me, it's going to be us. Because right now, I'm - listen, I am a beneficiary of capitalism. I have been wildly fortunate because of capitalism. But we are - we're going to lose capitalism unless we find a combination of a ways to address it. So what are the ways? The...

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: The investment accounts is a perfect way, right? Where you are building opportunity for the next generation. The um the school choice provision in the working families tax cut act. $1,700, everybody in this room can give $1,700 every year. Billions of dollars that are going to go into um school choice for for making sure everybody has equal quality of opportunity. If you don't do that at maximum scale and this moment leaves the majority of Americans behind, we're not going to be able to get anything done.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Spend more money on programs.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Yes, spend more money, but not spend more money, but spend more money in giving people opportunities. So the reason those two accounts are great, it takes it out of the government, it circumvents the government. It's based on incentives and uh choice by people that have wealth. Um it's uh it's sort of Andrew Carnegie uh brought to present day. Take that wealth, put it in the hands of those who will benefit from it, but it's your choice. It's not dict- dictated by the government. I think that's going to have to be part of the answer.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Does anyone in Congress subscribe to the notion that I think is rooted in you know um empiricism that the more we spend, the harder it is for people to have economic mobility? You know, and I think that there's a simplified notion that government has to play a role in driving people's progress. And the truth is, the more government intervenes in markets, the more inefficient and the more expensive those markets get, and therefore the more inaccessible wealth and value creation is for any individual. And the freer the market, the less the government intervenes, the less the government is spending, the less the government is buying, the better things get for people generally speaking. And it seems to me every time I visit DC, I leave profoundly unhappy because I meet with people in Congress and this is a notion that seems to be diametrically opposed to their viewpoints that everything is about doing more for people, government needs to do more on both sides of the aisle versus if the government did less, things would get more affordable, things would get more accessible and people would have greater economic mobility.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Well, I mean, you know, talk about the the the government. Now, we had not just one, we had two shutdowns. Both were historic in terms of their length. You know, we couldn't just agree on very basic things. And now we've shut the government down collectively for over uh 120 days or whatever it is now. And that's one of the things as a Democrat I refuse to engage in that thing. If that's a core responsibility as a senator, keep our government open and for the love of God, find a way to agree enough to keep things open and put things at risk and make our country less less safe. You know, um proud to be a capitalist. And uh capitalism is the the one system that has proven to raise lives' standard of humans across the globe, you know, that. And now people in my party or now...

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Now we're having bad ideas refused to die and now we're talking about socialism and communism right now. We have, we have candidates now that, 'I am a communist', these things. And it's like, mmm, that seems interesting. You know, what's your point now? Talk to anyone that's had to live under those kinds of regime. Now, they're all absolute capitalists right now and that's, that's absolutely the fact. That the market will has fixed a lot of these kinds of problems, you know, and I'm not terrified by AI.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Am I concerned about it? 100% right now and whether the myths and and other concerns about mass hacking and weaponizing it that way, you know, that's coming and now I think that should belong to America. You know, that, you know, America should build that chassis. And now that's why if we turn my party into where AI is the scourge or the cancer, now I refuse to reject that because it's going to transform the world.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Now, it can come from us or it can come from China. Whose side are you gonna be in? And a lot of the people that oppose that, now, a lot of that opposition is funded by a lot of these groups that are aligned with CCP. So that's part of the danger right now in town. So that's a dangerous time right now. And like Democrats now, my party, oh, the problems are billionaires. Oh, but we love the billionaires that fund, you know, our kinds of views and our views and all that too.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Uh, I don't target people, you know, people, you know, call out Bezos or whatever. It's like, you don't, don't do that. Don't call out private citizens, identify them as the problem. You know, they create jobs. If you've never created a job, don't criticize people that that have really changed our, our economy for the better, I honestly.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Why is Graham Platner doing so well in the polls? This is a candidate that has a literal Nazi tattoo on his chest.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Yeah, no.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And what does history tell us comes next if he wins? If a candidate like that can win, what does that tell us?

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well when I, when I was a kid, if someone had a Nazi tattoo, you probably could call them a Nazi sympathizer, you know? And so someone now, and now just yesterday we discovered that he was sexting with up to a dozen women. And now Democrats now, some are saying, well what's the big problem, you know? That's, you know, they're constantly just ignoring a lot of these things.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Um, and now this is a guy that just a couple of years, a couple years ago described an American soldier in a firefight with the Taliban... 'dumb motherfucker that doesn't deserve to live'. That's his words. Who does that? Who lurks on there and you know he's really a tough guy behind a keyboard and I can't explain other than that it's a backlash to how partisan things are, someone like that should have been in this race right now.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Now, uh, so here we are, you know, a guy that was text- sexting up to a dozen women, you know, he's getting his swallow on, and now we're kind of just, oh, what's the big deal? Why not confront that and just call that out? And that's- that's the danger if now these- these kinds of views...

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Could you imagine- could you imagine if either one of us described an American soldier as a dumb motherfucker that deserves to die? Or he described the- our army as absolute trash, absolute trash? He described American army as absolute trash. Um, and that's a viable candidate now, and that's- that's a bizarre place to live right now as a Democrat.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: If he wins, what happens? What does that tell us about where we're headed?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Well look at what- I mean what's happening there is- I mean there's problems on both parties, but certainly what you've seen on the left is two things. I think it's indisputable. One is a migration to these terrible ideas of socialism and Marxism and so forth. But in addition to that, this really rise of anti-semitism and hatred. And the tell here of how pervasive it is, is the people who are now campaigning in Maine, Chuck Schumer, Elizabeth Warren, these are mainstream Democrats that have now lurched so far to the left. And I think- I think this is going to be- if those candidates win and that becomes validation of the viability of that stream of thinking in the Democratic party, I think it's entirely horribly unhealthy for America. Ironically the best thing we want if we're Republicans is a strong Democratic party that keeps us honest and keeps us in check with good ideas. And uh and that's not happening. And uh as I said we have problems on both sides, but I think that lurch to the left is really frightening, a frightening thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Well and let's face- and you just brought up Israel.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: You know, that's a profound betrayal from our party on- on Israel. You know, the people that are winning a lot of these elections, you know, one of their signature view is anti-anti-Israel and borderline just, you know, raw anti-semitism from a lot of these candidates now too. And that's- that's exactly where we- where we are. Um and now that has isolated me in the party and just calling that what that is. I mean that's- that's dangerous. And now uh that's where- one of the things that we happen to agree on. Uh that's the right side of history for me.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: I mean maybe one of the best things that can happen is Shapiro continues to do well in Pennsylvania. Irrespective of which party you align with, a prominent Jew winning in Pennsylvania, running that state well is a very good sign actually for the rest of the country that there's common ground and that anti-semitism is-

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Josh is much more in check than the perception. Shapiro we had also on...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Josh I think is fantastic.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: And he, you know, has a lot of disagreement about us maybe going into this war and you know, maybe being egged into doing it.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And by the way, Fetterman, if you're looking or Friedberg, if you're looking for the source of why people feel comfortable saying these terrible things, you can look no further than Trump and what he said about McCain being a war hero.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That started this actual, this rhetoric, and now everybody wants to one-up it. He said he's not a war hero and he got captured. That's where this started. Sacks?

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: Oh please. You're gonna blame Trump for the fact that a Democratic candidate in Maine has a swastika tattooed on his arm?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No, the rhetoric. No, the rhetoric. It started with Trump.

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: We're in TDS land. We're in TDS land. But fortunately we're not interviewing you, J-Cal.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Let me ask Senator Fetterman a question. So I appreciate your common sense views on AI, on capitalism, on all the things we're talking about. And I think that the people in Pennsylvania appreciate it. I think you're very popular there. But I'm curious, do you ever get worried that you might get primaried in the Democratic Party when you come up? Because I mean the Bernie Sanders wing is powerful, they have a lot of activists, they're very noisy. I mean, I appreciate your maverick streak and your independence, but how do you think about that risk?

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Well, a seat or a job is is not- I'm always going to be- I'm going to be honest and I'm always going to have, you know, my votes or my opinions are what I happen to believe is true. You know, it's not a seat worth trying to maintain if I'm going to lie or to pretend that things aren't absolutely true. Yeah, absolutely, I can be primaried and there are some Democrats that are angry at me. If they're angry at me for supporting Israel, hey, they have at it. If they're angry at me that I think it's wrong to shut down the government, that's fine. Um, if if they're angry at me because I I refuse to call him a Nazi or a fascist or a piece of shit, I'll never do that, you can hold me on that too. But what's really strange and the the truth is, somebody that votes like a 93% of Democratic line and if I changed my party, there was a lot of policy intrigue that I'm going to change the party and I'm not, I'm never going to do that. And if I did I could change right now, I am changing it right now, my votes aren't going to change, my views- excuse me, I wouldn't vote any differently things I happen to believe. And and if if Democrats run me out of the party, they will be attacking a committed Democrat that votes your line 93% of the time because we're in a unique time right now that I think things are...

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: more important than trying to I I know what pays the bills as a Democrat now literally campaigns are based on fuck Trump literally in their campaign commercials yes and in their emails and outrage you know it's constant rage bait I refuse to engage in that and I'm going to be an example of what I think's entirely appropriate and and a lot of the conversations are driven by people that represent very you know safe blue states now like but that's why Pennsylvania keeps people honest here and people are going to remember in 28 oh yeah oh I want to be bipartisan I want to get along you know like people don't believe you you know they remember what what they've said and how people behave so that's where I'm at so yeah I could be primaryed but what's more important to be the truthful what I think's are important values and remind people that the things we used to believe in you know I I think

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Senator I want to I want to I want to just move to just domestic policy and economics for a second. You mentioned this incredible story in the green room which maybe you can start off with which is the quantum of people that reach out to you every week to mostly complain about various companies but just use that as an insight maybe to talk about AI because that's a huge part of your state it's also a huge part of the American economy and maybe compare it to what happened with shale and oil because there's a lot of commonalities I think it's quite interesting.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Yeah it's uh I don't know if he gets the same amount but I on an average week we have 14 million people in Pennsylvania almost on an average week I'll get something like 100,000 outreaches to me letters emails phone calls across different issues and you can almost see when the activists are mobilizing groups to come and make the case on certain things and I do a call every two weeks it's sort of an open mic where you get about 10,000 people to show up and the the number of data center calls data centers have become a much bigger and pervasive thing and just to put this in perspective David was there Dina and I hosted an energy and innovation summit in Pennsylvania last July we had 92 billion dollars of investment committed we had the energy CEOs the AI CEOs big investors global investors we had Senator Fetterman came governor Shapiro came the president came the cabinet came it was amazing and that's by the way that's what you need winning is a team sport and winning in AI is a team sport and you need all those players at the table um and so it was a real success and since then there's been enormous investments made in Pennsylvania I visited

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: I just the other week, Homer City, 4.4 gigawatts of power from a coal plant that's being transitioned to natural gas. 3.4 gigs is going to go into a data center complex, a gigawatt's going back on the grid to hopefully bring down energy prices. So this is, this is happening across Pennsylvania, but the opposition is growing and it's clear...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Is there any organic part to this? Like is this, because it's very hard to understand what's happening when you have these kind of dark forces, dark pools of money. I can't... is there a legitimate like 'I don't want to have this in my backyard for X, Y, and Z'?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Two things. There's enormous misinformation and I think that misinformation is largely being driven by China and outside forces. And then there's just legitimate not-understanding, not-knowing. And we need to do a much better job of making sure that the outlines of what these data centers mean to communities is clear. And, and frankly, there needs to be a much clearer covenant of what it means. If you're going to have a data center in your community and you're a township supervisor, here's what you know: um, you're going to they're going to bring more energy than they're going to use, they're going to protect their water with closed loop, um they're going to give this much to the tax base, they're going to build schools or infrastructure, whatever they're going to build.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Isn't that first part the only thing that matters in people's minds? 'I just don't want my energy bill to go up'?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: No. Um, because as soon as you get through that, they move to the next thing, which is water. And then the schools are going to be crowded and there's like so...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The schools are going to be crowded?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Yeah, because there'll be new jobs? No, because there'll be new people and they're not, they're not making the connection to the tax base. The roads are going to be crowded. So there's a whole comprehensive case that needs to be made. And, what Chamath was making the point, we went through this before, John and I both lived through this with fracking. Huge campaign against, huge misinformation, lack of understanding, and 15 years later I'd say there's pretty broad-base support for fracking.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well, no, yeah, there's unlimited money. I mean, you know, they, they someone finds some B-roll kind of a comment and they put $10 million behind it and they can, they can turn you into the most extreme kinds of things. It's ironically, you know, originally people tried to convince people that I'm a socialist, and now people are like, now I'm MAGA or whatever. I'm like, no, I'm just I haven't changed. My views are, my views are, are the, the same. And, you know, I would tell people energy is national security. Look at what's happening in, you know, in, in Europe and other parts now if, if you don't have energy security, then your, your national security is in danger. Now, AI is coming. And, and what, what he's done, he created this amazing symposium to remind people that these, you know, we need both. We need energy and Pennsylvania produces a lot of that. And now also AI is coming otherwise we'd still be using the spinny Jenny or whatever that's called, you know. So it's why do we talk about data centers...

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Because people, forces are describing them as a scourge or a cancer that's going to bankrupt you or destroy your community. Why do we have that right now? Because this is something that's going to transform our society. Absolutely there could be a downside, but overall it's going to be much more productive and that's going to create new opportunities. Now, I refuse to become a party of Luddites. And now that's honest. If I had to vote now and claim that that's all cancer, it's danger, and it's all downside, then I'd be lying and that's why I'm saying, Republican or Democrat, you know, that's a fact. These things are very, very important and that's why I'm going to—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Can I just double-click on this thing when you compared it to shale? So, in shale, I'm going to assume that when shale was escalating in importance, the pushback were from corporate interests that would have economically lost out. That's what I'm guessing. The majority—?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: That's what I'm guessing.

full_transcript.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
full_transcript.text: Environmentalists.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Environmental.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Well if you go to those two parts, one was the campaign of misinformation and the other is a lack of understanding. There's much more of a campaign of misinformation from outside forces this time around. But but what happened in the in the case of shale? Methane, destroys the water table, you know, don't want these in our backyards. What happened of course was these enormous royalties for the farmers. We're the fourth largest natural gas reserves in the world. If we were a country, Pennsylvania would be number four. This is the economic engine for our state. Our state is the second largest energy producer in the country. So this unlocks everything for Pennsylvania. And as those jobs started to be created, so you saw the economic benefit, the misinformation about the methane and the water table became clear, slowly and much too long, 15 years later we're now in a place where I bet 80% of people would support fracking.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Last night event, you know, you described that we have six to eight months ahead of China, right? Is that right? Yeah. Okay, so now, you know, my party is driving for a moratorium for data centers. Like, I mean, do you think China doesn't love that? You know like and that's why they're they're part of driving some of this this view. You want to give that race to China? It's coming. It's coming. It's the choice. Do you want America to build that chassis or do you want the Chinese to do that? You know, like, yeah, you know data centers can make some things with some some—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And at the core of that, John, is what you said earlier, which is it starts with TDS and then it goes with 'give China AI'. Is that— is it as reductively simple as that?

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: I would say this, vote me out if you think it's okay to give that race to—

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: You're gonna give China a benefit to win the AI race.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Vote me out! You know, and you know, but it's like so...

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Your favorite person in the audience, my wife is clapping for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Yeah, no but exactly. Exactly. That's exactly the way I see it. I mean, this is exactly the way I see it as well. So, um, we have great bipartisan agreement here.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: So Dave, I was at your summit last year, the Energy and Innovation Summit where you announced 90 billion dollars of new investments. The thing I was struck by was how broad-based the representation was at that summit. You didn't just have software companies, you had hardware companies, you had manufacturing, you had robotics, you had energy, nuclear, fracking. And then most interestingly is that you had the trades there. And that's when a light bulb went on for me that oh, this is a blue collar boom because this is a construction boom and blue collar workers are really gonna benefit and the trades who represented there spoke to that. And since then we've had hundreds of thousands of new jobs, construction jobs created and 25 to 30 percent wage increases for construction workers. And so I'm curious, does that help make the cause more popular in Pennsylvania? Are those trades, are the unions still on board with this or have the politics of that changed at all over the past year?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: It's a great question. And it's been extraordinary. So just in the last 15-16 months, and these are related by the way, it's not just energy projects and data centers, it's US Steel and a next generation mill, it's Eli Lilly building a new plant, it's Hanwha in the Philadelphia shipyard. But they're coming in part because of the data centers and the energy projects. And so I've spent a lot of time with the trades lately. The most secure job in Pennsylvania right now is a secure welder. I mean, is a seasoned welder.

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: And electrician.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: And electrician. These, honestly, these folks are coming out, they're getting the training, they're in their late teens, early 20s. They're making more than 100 grand a year in these sites and they can't build, they can't hire quick enough. They can't find enough people to meet the demand. And you know these the data centers, I mean I've looked closely at them, there's one you know coming in Berwick, Pennsylvania right next to where my where I went to high school. You know the the way to think about it is there's sort of four buckets of economic activity that comes, jobs that come. There's of course the deals that come with them, but there's the thousands of jobs to build the data centers and the energy that goes side by side. So these are 5, 10,000 folks on the site. Then there's the hundreds of people to run the data centers. Then of course every 3 to 4 years the the hardware and everything in the data centers gets upgraded which brings in hundreds, sometimes thousands of jobs. There's the energy that's going side by side. Then there's the restaurants, the hotels. I met with the the trucking association the other day, they they guess that it's about two logistics jobs...

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: ...for every one job in a data center, these things are economic engines. So once you get that covenant straight, people are willing to make the decision. And if a township supervisor or a county commissioner, no Senator is going to tell them to do this. This has to come from the ground up in terms of making the decision. But once they decide to do that, it's a huge engine for their communities. If they decide not to do it, my point is, okay, what's your plan? What is the plan? In Pennsylvania, we've been losing people for 20 years, we can't get our kids to stay. This is like a rebirth, it's amazing what's happening. But we got to break through the misinformation.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Friedberg, you have a final question? If not, I'll...

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: Well, I mean, I think on this point, why do you think it's so difficult for the Democrats to embrace the facts on the ground? Because you have an empirical story to share about job creation, value creation, and there's no empiricism on the other side that there's job displacement or job loss in the white-collar jobs, which is generally part of the narrative.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Well, two things. I would say the notion of AI broadly is getting munged with the notion of data centers in a narrow thing. Data centers, of course, are an enormous component of this, but those two, those two things are unrelated. Second, I don't think it's just Democrats. Like, I mean, we have...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No, the America First movement is now in this horseshoe exactly aligned with the Democrats.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: We have the far left and the far right converging on an anti-

full_transcript.speaker: David Sacks
full_transcript.text: We even have some besties who occasionally buy into the jobs apocalypse.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No, no, I'm... I'm job displacement. Sacks, don't ever misrepresent me. I will call you out every time. I just believe there'll be job...

full_transcript.speaker: David Friedberg
full_transcript.text: You could also use the word job mobility.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What I... I'll let finish my sentence please. I'm speaking. Job displacement is occurring and that new jobs need to be created to fill that in. And I think that's the blind spot of the people who have Trump dedication syndrome is that they will never actually do anything that would modestly criticize the President. And that's part of the challenge. I want to have a last question just about the amount of money being donated by special interest. Do we need to get money out of politics? Because that seems to be the root of a lot of these problems is that, you know, billionaires can give 50 million, 100 million, 200 million, whether it's Soros or friends of ours. It feels like there's way too much money in politics. Does that have to change for this type of issue, you know, around data centers etc. and special interest? And how do we... can we ever get there?

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well, I... I would say of the five things I would do to fix America right now, that wouldn't be in the top five. But yes, conceptually I'd like to get money out of politics. There was... my race was 500 million dollars. We raised 200 million on my side, 300 million on the opposition side. We couldn't keep up.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: 500 million! I was record before in '22. We raised, you know, and...

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: And now, my race was 300-330 mil, and and now that's quaint. Wait 'til '26. Wait 'til '28.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: This is gonna look like quaint. And and that's the thing. You know, collectively...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: We either need to get money out or this group needs to be donating a lot more to both of you.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Yeah, or get get rid of the primary process too. You know? And and now open to other people.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: And now these primaries, they identify the extreme kinds of views and put out someone, you know, and that's part of it now too. Collectively, $300 million were spent to describe- to destroy our reputations.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Think what $300 million could do for Pennsylvania or for people. That was spent to destroy reputations. You know, you're terrible, I'm terrible. That's the absurdity of the American politics right now.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: On that note, I actually want to say both of you are incredibly rational and reasonable and incredible Americans. So I just want to thank you. Senators Fetterman and McCormick.

full_transcript.speaker: John Fetterman
full_transcript.text: Very much appreciate it.

full_transcript.speaker: Dave McCormick
full_transcript.text: Thank you. Great to see you.

highlights: Senators John Fetterman and Dave McCormick emphasize a "country over party" approach, arguing that finding common ground on consequential issues like energy and AI is essential to overcoming national polarization.

Both senators advocate for the rapid expansion of AI and data centers as a national security priority, framing the technological race as a choice between American leadership and a "China-first" future.
Senator Fetterman notably reversed his previous stance on the filibuster, describing it as a critical tool for governance that protects minority rights and forces both parties to work together.

The discussion highlights the growing anxiety of working-class families regarding the "K-shaped economy," suggesting that current wealth concentration requires new pathways for social mobility and local investment.

The senators critiqued the "absurdity" of modern political spending, noting that hundreds of millions of dollars are often spent on "rage bait" and destroying reputations rather than addressing substantive issues for constituents.
summary:
This transcript features a rare joint appearance by Pennsylvania’s senators, Democrat John Fetterman and Republican Dave McCormick, who emphasize the necessity of bipartisanship and "country over party." They describe Pennsylvania as a microcosm of the United States, where the diverse needs of urban and rural voters force politicians to find common ground on consequential issues like energy, national security, and economic mobility. Both senators reject the "rage bait" and tribalism of modern politics, arguing that their ability to work together—despite their ideological differences—is essential for the state’s role as a leader in the national economy.

A central theme of the discussion is the strategic intersection of energy production and artificial intelligence. McCormick and Fetterman view AI not as a threat, but as a "blue-collar boom" that can revitalize Pennsylvania’s industrial sector through the construction and operation of data centers. They argue that leveraging the state’s natural gas reserves to power the AI revolution is a matter of national security, warning that internal opposition to data centers effectively serves a "China-first" policy. Fetterman specifically criticizes members of his own party who view AI as a "scourge," framing it instead as the next great chassis for American innovation and job creation for tradespeople like welders and electricians.

The conversation also addresses the systemic failures of modern governance, including the ballooning national debt, the high cost of elections, and the erosion of institutional trust. In a notable moment of self-reflection, Fetterman admits he was wrong to previously support ending the filibuster, now arguing that it is a vital safeguard that forces the Senate toward compromise. Both men express frustration with the $500 million spent on their respective campaigns, lamenting that such vast resources are used primarily to destroy reputations rather than solve intractable problems. Ultimately, they advocate for a return to rational conduct and truth-telling as the only path toward rebuilding the American middle class and maintaining a competitive edge on the global stage.

(0:33) How Bipartisanship Actually Works in 2025
(6:38) Why the Filibuster Is the Last Line of Defense Against Total Tribalism
(13:50) The K-Shaped Economy Is a Ticking Time Bomb and Both Parties Know It
(19:55) When Extremism Wins Primaries: What It Means for the Future of American Politics
(28:38) AI and Energy Are Creating a Blue Collar Boom and Pennsylvania Is Proof
(39:00) Getting Money Out of Politics and Whether Bipartisanship Can Survive 2028

The role and preservation of the Senate filibuster
Economic inequality and the challenges of the K-shaped economy

The impact of political extremism and campaign finance on American democracy
